reason: Both answers cover the core points well: push back but narrowly, cap liability around 12 months of fees, narrow but keep IP indemnity with standard carve-outs and a remedy ladder, avoid walking away over negotiable boilerplate, get a lawyer redline, consider E&O insurance, and frame the ask as standard early-stage vendor terms. They are similar in clarity and actionability. B adds a correct and useful nuance about standard carve-outs from the liability cap (vendor indemnity obligations, confidentiality breach, gross negligence) and flags a few extra real items (auto-renewal, termination for convenience, warranty disclaimers). A has a minor inaccuracy suggesting unlimited liability could bankrupt founders personally via the company, which blurs the corporate liability shield of a Delaware C-corp. This gives B a small edge.
